canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, WikiQA is consistently described as a benchmark for evaluating question answering (QA) capabilities. It is explicitly used to measure both general "Question answering" and more specific "Open-ended question answering". The application of the benchmark varies across studies, with several papers using it to assess answer selection performance from a set of candidate answers. A smaller line of work applies it to "closed-book QA," as noted in one paper ("Merge, Then Compress: Demystify Efficient SMoE with Hints from Its Routing Policy"). Another recurring use is for evaluating "spoken-query-based question answering (SQQA)," sometimes with synthesized speech inputs. A different framing focuses on the output format, where one study uses WikiQA for "sentence-level responses in calibration evaluation," observing that on this benchmark, "model responses are complete sentences" ("LitCab: Lightweight Language Model Calibration over Short- and Long-form Responses").
papers: LitCab: Lightweight Language Model Calibration over Short- and Long-form Responses
Merge, Then Compress: Demystify Efficient SMoE with Hints from Its Routing Policy
SALMONN: Towards Generic Hearing Abilities for Large Language Models
Follow My Instruction and Spill the Beans: Scalable Data Extraction from Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems
HaDeMiF: Hallucination Detection and Mitigation in Large Language Models
Evaluating Small Language Models for News Summarization: Implications and Factors Influencing Performance
Knowledge Graph Guided Evaluation of Abstention Techniques
